content.text:
{Unless the Lord builds the house, those who build it labor in vain.}{Psalm 127:1}
 {The object of mathematical rigor is to sanction and legitimize the conquests of intuition.}{— Jacques Hadamard}

Just as integers can be factored into primes, matrices can be factored 
into products of simpler matrices. These **matrix factorizations** 
reveal structure and enable efficient computation.

content.title: Elementary Matrix
content.content:
An **elementary matrix** is a matrix 
obtained by performing a single elementary row operation on the 
identity matrix.

content.text: There are three types, corresponding to the three row operations:

items:
**Row replacement:** $E$ has a $1$ in position $(i,j)$ 
 with $i \neq j$ (adds row $j$ to row $i$).

**Row scaling:** $E$ has a nonzero entry other than $1$ 
 on the diagonal.

content.title: Elementary Matrices and Row Operations
content.content:
If $E$ is the elementary matrix for a row operation, then $EA$ gives 
the same result as applying that operation to $A$.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Elementary matrices
content.problem:
The operation "add 3 times row 2 to row 1" on a $3 \times 3$ matrix 
corresponds to:
\[
E = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\]
Then $EA$ performs this operation on $A$.

content.text: Every elementary matrix is invertible, and its inverse is also elementary:

items:
The inverse of "add $k$ times row $j$ to row $i$" is "add 
 $-k$ times row $j$ to row $i$."
The inverse of "interchange rows $i$ and $j$" is itself.

The inverse of "multiply row $i$ by $k$" is "multiply row $i$ 
 by $1/k$."

content.title: LU Factorization
content.content:
An **LU factorization** of a matrix $A$ 
is a representation $A = LU$ where:

- $L$ is **lower triangular** (all entries above the diagonal 
 are zero) with ones on the diagonal.

- $U$ is **upper triangular** (all entries below the diagonal 
 are zero) and is in echelon form.

content.text:
The LU factorization (when it exists) is extremely useful for solving 
systems. To solve $A\vect{x} = \vect{b}$:

content.title: Finding an LU factorization
When LU fails
content.content:
Not every matrix has an LU factorization. If row interchanges are 
needed during elimination, we need a permutation matrix $P$ such that 
$PA = LU$. This is the **PLU factorization**.

content.title: Invertibility and Elementary Matrices
content.content:
An $n \times n$ matrix $A$ is invertible if and only if $A$ is a 
product of elementary matrices.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
If $A$ is invertible, row reduction transforms $A$ to $I$: 
$E_k \cdots E_1 A = I$. Thus $A = E_1\inv \cdots E_k\inv$, a product 
of elementary matrices.

marginNotes.text:
For no one can lay a foundation 
other than that which is laid, which is Jesus Christ.
